links.title: Linux Documentation Project
links.description: Comprehensive Linux documentation and guides for beginners and advanced users.

links.title: DistroWatch
links.description: News, popularity rankings, and information about Linux distributions.

links.title: Linux Journey
links.description: Learn the ways of Linux-fu, from beginner to system administrator.

links.title: It's FOSS
links.description: Latest Linux news, apps, software updates, and tutorials.

links.title: Linux Command Library
links.description: A searchable database of Linux commands with examples and explanations.

links.title: Arch Wiki
links.description: Extensive documentation for Arch Linux and many other distributions.

tips.title: Verify Your Downloads
tips.content: Always verify the integrity of downloaded ISO files using checksums (MD5, SHA256) before installation to ensure they haven't been tampered with.

tips.title: Use Timeshift for Backups
tips.content: Timeshift is a powerful backup tool available on many Linux distributions. Create system snapshots before major updates or changes.

tips.title: Try Before You Install
tips.content: Most Linux distributions offer a 'live' mode that lets you try the OS without installing. Boot from USB and test compatibility with your hardware.

tips.title: Keep Your System Updated
tips.content: Regular updates improve security and stability. Use your distribution's package manager to keep your system up to date.

tips.title: Learn Basic Terminal Commands
tips.content: Familiarize yourself with basic commands like ls, cd, mkdir, and apt/dnf/pacman. The terminal is powerful and often faster than GUI tools.

tips.title: Check Hardware Compatibility
tips.content: Before choosing a distribution, check hardware compatibility, especially for Wi-Fi cards, graphics cards, and peripherals.

tips.title: Use Local Mirrors
tips.content: Configure your package manager to use mirrors geographically close to you for faster downloads and updates.

tips.title: Customize Your Desktop
tips.content: Linux offers extensive customization options. Explore different desktop environments (GNOME, KDE, XFCE) to find what works best for you.

links.title: TempleOS Archive
links.description: An operating system built by Terry A. Davis in HolyC. Designed to be a modern Commodore-64 and a divine temple.

links.title: KolibriOS
links.description: Tiny, fast OS written in assembly. Boots in seconds, fits on a floppy disk, and includes a GUI, text editor, and more.

links.title: Haiku OS
links.description: Open-source continuation of BeOS—designed for speed, efficiency, and simplicity with a native GUI.

links.title: Serenity OS
links.description: A beautiful UNIX-like OS built from scratch. Clean C++ codebase, custom GUI, and growing developer following.

links.title: ReactOS
links.description: Windows-compatible open-source OS aimed at binary-level compatibility with Windows apps and drivers.

links.title: Plan 9 from Bell Labs
links.description: Experimental OS from Bell Labs focused on distributed computing and a unified file interface. Very influential in systems design.

tips.title: Try in a VM First
tips.content: Many alternative OSes aren’t suited for real hardware. Use VirtualBox, QEMU, or GNOME Boxes to test them safely.

tips.title: Expect the Unexpected
tips.content: Most alt OSes don’t follow Linux conventions—don’t expect systemd, apt, or bash. Embrace the weird.

tips.title: Join Small Communities
tips.content: Alt OS communities are often small but passionate. Forums, IRC, and GitHub issues are great ways to get help.

tips.title: Look at the Source Code
tips.content: Many of these OSes are written from scratch. Browsing their code is a masterclass in kernel design and UI architecture.

tips.title: Host ISOs for the Future
tips.content: Preserve early versions of rare or discontinued OSes. You may be the last mirror one day.

tips.title: Use Read-Only Images
tips.content: Some of these OSes can corrupt disks or write in odd formats. Use ISO boot mode or snapshots to avoid issues.

links.title: Krita
links.description: Professional free and open-source painting program for digital artists, illustrators, and concept designers.

links.title: GIMP
links.description: The GNU Image Manipulation Program—powerful for photo retouching, image editing, and composition.

links.title: Inkscape
links.description: Free and open-source vector graphics editor for illustrators and designers—ideal for SVG workflows.

links.title: Kdenlive
links.description: A robust, multi-track non-linear video editor that works great on Linux and supports a wide range of codecs and effects.

links.title: Audacity
links.description: Popular multi-track audio editor and recorder used for podcasts, music editing, and voiceovers.

links.title: LMMS
links.description: A free cross-platform music production suite—create beats, synths, and full tracks with MIDI support.

tips.title: Use Flatpak for Creative Apps
tips.content: Creative tools like Krita and Inkscape often ship the latest versions as Flatpaks. They're sandboxed and easy to update.

tips.title: Color Calibrate Your Monitor
tips.content: For accurate digital painting or video editing, use tools like DisplayCAL and a hardware colorimeter.

tips.title: Edit Non-Destructively
tips.content: Use layer-based editing in apps like GIMP and Krita to retain flexibility when creating or retouching images.

tips.title: Try JACK Audio for Pro Audio
tips.content: Use JACK with tools like Ardour or Carla for low-latency, professional-grade Linux audio production setups.

tips.title: Use SVGs for Scalable Artwork
tips.content: Vector formats like SVG are ideal for logos, icons, and UI design. Inkscape is a great choice for editing them.

tips.title: Keep Backups of Your Projects
tips.content: Creative files are often irreplaceable. Use Timeshift, rsync, or cloud backup to keep your work safe.

links.title: Kiwix
links.description: Offline reader for web content—download and browse entire websites like Wikipedia or Stack Overflow without Internet access.

links.title: Zotero
links.description: Free tool to collect, organize, cite, and share research, with strong offline capabilities and local libraries.

links.title: Project Gutenberg
links.description: Over 70,000 free eBooks you can download and store locally—great for reading and archival projects.

links.title: LibreOffice
links.description: Powerful open-source office suite—word processing, spreadsheets, presentations—all fully offline-capable.

links.title: Joplin
links.description: Markdown-based note-taking and to-do app with full offline sync and encryption support.

links.title: ArchiveBox
links.description: Self-hosted web archiving solution to collect, save, and browse URLs offline, including full HTML snapshots.

tips.title: Build a Self-Contained Toolkit
tips.content: Use tools like Ventoy or GRUB2 to bundle multiple rescue ISOs, documentation, and apps onto a single USB drive.

tips.title: Mirror What You Rely On
tips.content: Sync copies of your most-used Linux repos, Flatpak apps, and docs using rsync for offline fallback or bandwidth-limited environments.

tips.title: Use Joplin with Local Sync
tips.content: You can run Joplin without cloud sync—use a shared folder or USB stick to move your notes between devices securely.

tips.title: Download Wikipedia with Kiwix
tips.content: Kiwix lets you download entire copies of Wikipedia or Wiktionary to a USB stick or internal drive—great for study or reference without Internet.

tips.title: Include System Repair Tools
tips.content: Don’t forget to add tools like GParted, Clonezilla, and SystemRescue to your offline kit for system emergencies.

tips.title: Store Digital Books Locally
tips.content: Use tools like Calibre to organize and read DRM-free eBooks offline. Works well with Project Gutenberg collections.

links.title: RetroArch
links.description: Frontend for emulators, game engines, and media players—an all-in-one solution for retro gaming.

links.title: DOSBox
links.description: A DOS emulator that lets you run old DOS games and applications flawlessly on modern systems.

links.title: ScummVM
links.description: Engine that allows you to play classic point-and-click adventure games like Monkey Island and Day of the Tentacle.

links.title: Lutris
links.description: Open gaming platform for Linux that helps you install and manage games from all kinds of sources—native, Wine, emulated, and more.

links.title: PICO-8
links.description: Fantasy console for creating, sharing, and playing tiny games—popular in the retro pixel art scene.

links.title: PlayOnLinux
links.description: Graphical frontend for Wine that helps you install and manage Windows games and software on Linux.

tips.title: Use Controllers with RetroArch
tips.content: RetroArch auto-detects many USB controllers and gamepads. Configure them in the input settings for smoother gameplay.

tips.title: Preserve Old Games
tips.content: Dump your old cartridge or CD-based games into ROMs or ISOs using tools like CloneCD or Retrode, and store them with checksums.

tips.title: Try DOSBox-X for Advanced Features
tips.content: DOSBox-X adds extended hardware support, save states, and better compatibility for DOS-based productivity tools.

tips.title: Build a Portable Gaming USB
tips.content: Combine tools like Ventoy and RetroArch with preloaded ROMs and emulators on a bootable USB for plug-and-play retro gaming.

tips.title: Explore Open-Source Remakes
tips.content: Many classic games have open-source remakes like OpenRA (Red Alert), FreeCiv, and OpenTTD. Great for LAN play or modding.

tips.title: Use Lutris for Game Management
tips.content: Lutris can manage and run GOG, Steam, emulators, and Wine games all in one launcher—perfect for organizing your retro setup.

links.title: Qubes OS
links.description: A security-focused operating system that uses Xen-based virtualization to compartmentalize tasks into isolated domains.

links.title: Tails OS
links.description: A live Linux system focused on privacy and anonymity, using Tor and leaving no trace on the machine you use.

links.title: Whonix
links.description: A desktop operating system designed for anonymity, utilizing Tor and virtualization to prevent IP leaks.

links.title: Tor Project
links.description: Official site for the Tor network and tools, including the Tor Browser for anonymous web access.

links.title: Proton VPN
links.description: A high-privacy VPN service from the makers of ProtonMail, offering a trustworthy free tier and no-logs policy.

links.title: Linux Security Checklist
links.description: Community-maintained hardening checklist for Linux desktops and laptops.

tips.title: Enable Full Disk Encryption
tips.content: Use LUKS or VeraCrypt to encrypt your drive, especially on laptops. This protects your data in case the device is lost or stolen.

tips.title: Use a Password Manager
tips.content: Avoid reusing passwords. Use open-source tools like Bitwarden or KeePassXC to securely store and manage your credentials.

tips.title: Avoid Using Root
tips.content: Run as a regular user and only use sudo when necessary. This reduces the chance of system-wide damage or compromise.

tips.title: Secure SSH Access
tips.content: Use SSH keys instead of passwords, disable root login, and change the default port to harden SSH access.

tips.title: Update Regularly
tips.content: Security patches are critical. Enable automatic security updates if your distro supports it.

tips.title: Use AppArmor or SELinux
tips.content: Mandatory access control systems like AppArmor or SELinux confine apps and reduce damage from misbehaving or malicious software.
